title: Expansion of the rhizosphere soil and phyllosphere bacterial resistomes following repeated farmland application of high-temperature granulation-treated antibiotic fermentation residues.
author_string: Wan, Ruiqi, Chen, Tianyu, Xun, Yanping, Chen, Yazhuo, Lv, Jitao et al.
abstract: Antibiotic fermentation residue (AFR), a nutrient-dense biosolid waste from the pharmaceutical industry, holds the potential for recycling as fertilizer. However, its high concentration of antibiotic residues poses a significant risk for the dissemination of antibiotic resistance within the ecosystem. In this study, industrial-scale high-temperature granulation (HTG) technology was tested for its ability to remove antibiotics from three bulk AFRs (penicillin, cephalosporin, and abamectin). Additionally, the impacts of repeated farmland application (3 years) of treated AFR on antibiotic residues and antibiotic resistance genes (ARGs) were assessed in the soil-plant system using liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry and high-throughput real-time quantitative PCR, respectively. Despite the effective removal of antibiotics (94 %-99 %), treated AFR caused the accumulation of antibiotics and ARGs in the soil and plant. The relative abundance of ARGs increased 141-fold in rhizosphere soil and 32-fold in the phyllosphere. Biomarker ARGs significantly associated with treated AFR fertilization were identified through Effect Size (LEfSE) analysis (11 in rhizosphere soil and 9 in the phyllosphere), with five of these being recognized as clinically relevant high-risk ARGs (cmlA1-01, mphA-01, catB3, lnuA-01, and ermB). Moreover, random forest regression models highlighted 9 biomarker ARGs (vgaB-01, tetS, aac(6')-Ib-1, blaOXA1/blaOXA30, aadA2-02, mphA-01, aphA1, aadD, and ermY) strongly associated with mobile genetic elements linked to anthropogenic activities. In conclusion, our results demonstrate the accumulation of antibiotics and significant expansion of the soil-plant antibiotic resistome following repeated farmland application of HTG-treated AFR, emphasizing the importance of evaluating the risk of treated AFR in agricultural application.
